content.text: Output consistency means Claude produces the same format, structure, and length for semantically equivalent inputs across multiple calls. Without deliberate enforcement, the model varies capitalization, list style, field presence, and verbosity based on subtle prompt differences. Consistent formatting is not a cosmetic concern — it is a correctness requirement for any downstream parser or UI that depends on predictable output shape.
Why Format Varies

Claude generates text token by token, conditioned on the prompt. Small variations in phrasing, word order, or context length shift the probability distribution over early tokens — and because formatting decisions (start with a list? use headers?) are made at the very beginning of the response, early variance propagates through the whole output. This is why identical-looking prompts can produce different structures on repeated calls.

The four most common sources of format drift are: (1) no explicit format specification in the system prompt, (2) few-shot examples that cover only the happy-path input shape, (3) instructions that are too abstract ("be structured") rather than concrete ("respond with a JSON object with these five fields"), and (4) no programmatic validation that rejects malformed output.
Layer 1: Explicit Format Constraints in the System Prompt

The system prompt is where format enforcement starts. Specify every dimension of the expected output: structure, field names, ordering, punctuation style, and length target. Vague instructions like "be concise" produce variable results; explicit targets like "respond in 2–3 sentences" are consistently enforced:

# Weak — leaves format decisions to the model
WEAK_SYSTEM = "Summarize the article concisely."

# Strong — specifies every format dimension
STRONG_SYSTEM = """
Summarize the article. Your response must follow this exact format:

Title: <article title, title case>
Summary: <2–3 sentences, plain prose, no bullet points>
Key points:
- <one bullet per key fact, 10 words max each>
- <second bullet>
Sentiment: <Positive | Neutral | Negative>

Do not add any text outside this structure.
"""

For structured output, showing the template rather than describing it is more effective. The model matches the template's shape, capitalization, and punctuation exactly rather than interpreting an abstract description.
Layer 2: Few-Shot Examples

Few-shot examples have the strongest effect on output format of any prompting technique. The model adapts to the format it sees in examples — capitalization, punctuation, field ordering, and prose style — even when the instruction text says something different. When instructions and examples conflict, examples usually win.

FEW_SHOT_SYSTEM = """
Classify customer support tickets.

## Format
Respond with JSON only. No prose before or after.

## Examples

Input: "My order hasn't arrived after 2 weeks."
Output: {"category": "shipping", "urgency": "high", "sentiment": "negative"}

Input: "How do I reset my password?"
Output: {"category": "account", "urgency": "low", "sentiment": "neutral"}

Input: "The product is amazing, 10/10!"
Output: {"category": "feedback", "urgency": "none", "sentiment": "positive"}
"""

# The examples fix: key names, value enums, quoting style, no trailing comma,
# no extra fields — all things an instruction description may leave ambiguous.

Cover edge cases in your few-shot examples. If your few-shot set only shows medium-length inputs and the model encounters a one-word query, it may default to a different format. Include short, long, and boundary-condition examples in the system prompt to prevent drift at the edges.
Layer 3: Structured Output with Schema Validation
For machine-readable output, request JSON explicitly and validate it with a schema. Invalid output fails fast rather than silently propagating malformed data downstream:
Layer 4: Post-processing Normalisation

Even a well-constrained model may produce minor format variations: trailing spaces, inconsistent newlines, markdown in a plain-text context, or mixed casing. Post-processing catches these without requiring a re-prompt:

import re

def normalize_output(text: str, expected_format: str = "plain") -> str:
    # Strip leading/trailing whitespace
    text = text.strip()

    if expected_format == "plain":
        # Remove markdown bold/italic
        text = re.sub(r'\*\*(.+?)\*\*', r'\1', text)
        text = re.sub(r'\*(.+?)\*', r'\1', text)
        # Remove markdown headers
        text = re.sub(r'^#{1,6}\s+', '', text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
        # Collapse multiple blank lines
        text = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', text)

    elif expected_format == "json":
        # Strip any prose wrapper the model may have added
        json_match = re.search(r'\{[\s\S]+\}', text)
        if json_match:
            text = json_match.group(0)

    elif expected_format == "list":
        # Normalise bullet style to single dash
        text = re.sub(r'^[•\*\+]\s+', '- ', text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
        # Normalise numbered list to period style: "1. "
        text = re.sub(r'^(\d+)[\)\.]\s+', r'\1. ', text, flags=re.MULTILINE)

    return text
Measuring Format Consistency

Consistency is measurable. Run the same prompt N times (typically 5–10) and compare the format across outputs. Two useful metrics are format compliance rate (fraction of outputs that match the expected schema) and inter-run consistency (fraction of pairs that have identical structure):
Common Format Drift Patterns
List vs. prose:
The model uses bullet points for detailed inputs and prose for short ones. Fix by specifying format independently of input length.
Header drift:
The model adds or drops section headers based on perceived complexity. Fix by enumerating every expected section in the system prompt template.
Field omission:
Optional-seeming fields are omitted when the model judges them irrelevant. Make all fields required, or explicitly specify "use null if not applicable."
Preamble insertion:
The model adds "Here is the summary:" before the structured output. Add "Do not include any text before or after the structured output" to the system prompt.
Length creep:
Responses grow longer on complex inputs. Enforce with explicit word or sentence counts rather than qualitative descriptors like "brief."
Using Prefill to Lock the Opening Format

One of the most reliable techniques for enforcing output format is prompt prefilling — placing the start of the expected output in the assistant turn before generation begins. Because the model continues from the prefill, the opening token is fixed and format drift at the beginning is eliminated:

def classify_with_prefill(text: str) -> dict:
    import json
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=128,
        system="Classify the support ticket. Respond with JSON only.",
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": text},
            # Prefill: forces the model to start mid-JSON object
            {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"category":'},
        ]
    )
    # Reconstruct full JSON from prefill + continuation
    raw = '{"category":' + resp.content[0].text
    return json.loads(raw)

# Prefill guarantees:
# - The response starts with a JSON object (no preamble)
# - The first key is always "category" (field ordering fixed)
# - No risk of markdown wrapper or explanatory text

Prefilling is particularly valuable when the output must begin with a specific keyword, field, or delimiter. It removes the sampling variance at position 0 — where format decisions are most consequential — without any additional prompt tokens.
Temperature and Format Consistency

Temperature controls the randomness of token selection. Higher temperature increases creative diversity but also increases format variance. For structured or format-critical outputs, use a low temperature:
Temperature
Format consistency
Best for
Maximum — near-deterministic
JSON classification, data extraction, structured parsing
High — format stable, content slightly varied
Summaries, Q&A with fixed format requirements
Lower — format variance increases
Creative writing, brainstorming — format less critical

Use temperature 0 for any output that is parsed programmatically. Even with a perfect system prompt, high temperature allows low-probability tokens to appear at format-critical positions (the opening brace, a field name), breaking parsers. Temperature 0 eliminates that risk by always selecting the highest-probability token.
Building a Format Regression Suite

Format requirements should be tested like functional requirements — as part of an automated regression suite that runs on every prompt change. A format regression test checks that the output structure, not just the content, matches expectations:

Run format regression tests whenever you change the system prompt, upgrade the model version, or modify post-processing logic. A prompt change that improves content quality while silently breaking the JSON structure is a regression, even if content metrics improve.
Summary
Specify format concretely in the system prompt: show a template, not just a description. Every unspecified dimension is a source of variance.
Use few-shot examples. They are the most effective format anchor and override abstract instructions when they conflict.
Request JSON for machine-readable output; validate with Pydantic or a schema. Invalid output = re-prompt, not silent pass.
Apply post-processing normalisation for plain-text outputs: strip markdown, collapse whitespace, normalise bullet style.
Measure consistency with format compliance rate and inter-run structural fingerprint comparison — run the same prompt N times as part of your eval suite.
